sentences.text: Well our name-- officially, our name was still Cohn.

But for checking, you know, because the authorities would every so often check you in the street and stop you and expect it and ask for your papers, you would always carry a false identity card for that-- for that purpose, and so we wouldn't get arrested.
My brother managed to go to the lycee.
And finally in Macon, which was the nearest city, but which was more than 10 kilometers away, he had an old bicycle.
And it took him like an hour riding his bicycle to school and then back.
And it was very difficult.
I remember in winter when he came back a couple of times covered with icicles, you know, his entire body.
So he had to be very dedicated.
It was not easy.
And I didn't even have a bicycle at this time, or my bicycle broke down every so often.
And I managed to go to school eventually.
There was a convent in our village, Carmelites.
And my father went to the mother superior one day and told her about me.
She was also an artist.
And he showed her some of my drawings.
And she said oh, yes, she has talent.
I would like to teach her, give her lessons.
And also why don't you send her to our school?
They had a private school, very exclusive, a girl's school.
They where from mostly aristocratic-- French aristocratic families.
Of course, none of- they were Catholic, very strict Catholics.
And she told my father-- she knew I was Jewish-- and she told my father, don't tell anybody you are Jewish.
Nobody has to know.
Officially she will be a Catholic.
Maybe they had some hopes, I mean, that I would convert or something, I don't know.
But any- whatever it was, it gave me a chance to go to school.
But the problem was that I had missed almost two years of school.
Plus it was a new language for me, because here I was in school in Belgium all in Flemish, and there it was French.
I knew some French, but I was not fluent yet.
And I had a little difficulty.
One of the nuns who was from Belgium, occasionally she would help me out.
She would come and kind of translate for me.
But very-- very- very fast I caught up, again, because I was motivated.
I had to work very hard.
And I fit in.
So I was actually the best in religion.
And I was the best in French history, among others.
And I remember the nun saying, telling the class that they should be ashamed, you know, as French, that a foreigner was better in French history than they are.
She didn't say the same thing about religion, because she didn't know I was Jewish.
Was there somebody there, your own mother or whomever, who helped give you some guidelines as to how to act non-Jewish or certain traditions?
No, because my mother was not Catholic.
She knew as little as I did.
And I remember I was in pure panic when one day, the nun came in and said this afternoon, there's such and such priest is here.
We're all going to confession.
And I thought, what am I going to do?
And I couldn't even ask anybody, you know, what do you do, what prayer do you say or whatever.
I had to just handle it.
So I tried to check it out with a prayer book, you know, checking out what you say before confession, what you do, what you-- so I invented some-- some things I did, you know.
Because I didn't know what to say.
And apparently I must have done the right thing, because he was not suspicious.
So he gave me some Our Fathers and some Hail Marys to recite afterwards, and that's it.
Now, after you moved out of the motel that you lived in and you moved to this village, where were you living, in what kind of situation?
We had a little house-- or it was actually very picturesque-- that had been abandoned.
It was like a tiny little farm or something like that.
It had been abandoned for ages.
Nobody had lived there.
And it was the most primitive thing you can imagine.
No plumbing, of course no running water, no electricity, nothing.
Just the four walls.
But they were very old.
They had walls about this-- this thick.
They're indestructible.
And they never do any repair to these places.
You know, they just last forever.
And that was it.
So no-- no toilet, no bathroom.
And we had to get the water from a neighbor farm from a well.
So it was a big job.
You know, you had to-- you had to-- first you had to pour water down the well, and then you had to-- there was a lever, and you had to-- you had to turn.
It was pretty hard to do.
And it was a big job.
So you had to get water that way.
But you know, you adjust.
That was the least of our-- of our worries.
See, the priorities are completely turned around.
They're very different.
It's not important.
Now I was 13, 14.
I would say 14.
So I was a teenager already.
Well, of course there wasn't much room for-- for fun.
So I would go to school.
Luckily I had the school.
I would start drawing as much as I could, painting, which was a problem, because you didn't even get paper.
There was nothing available.
So it was very primitive what you did.
And I had-- real friends, I didn't have.
We would-- we would work with the farmers a lot in our spare time.
I think that's one of the reasons also we survived, because we would blend in and we would help them as much as we could.
And they realized, you know, that we were-- eventually they would accept us as one of them.
You know, we were just part of-- which was kind of unusual I would say, because these farmers, they are very-- they cut themselves off completely.
Each village actually, each village is like a separate world.
They may as well- They're surrounded by an invisible wall.
And people from another village, already they look at them with suspicion.
You know, they're like foreigners.
They don't trust them.
So they accepted [BACKROUND NOISE] us as almost like family, as part of them.
And we would participate.
We would even at night, quite often, like in winter, since they didn't have television, obviously, they would say come over, you know, tonight.
And well, they would just sit and talk or play chess, or you know, they served something.
And that's the way you-- you would spend nights, winter nights or whatever.
So we felt like we were almost part of them.
We could have stayed there forever.
Actually, they-- they- they were a little disappointed when we left eventually.
You know, I think they wanted us to stay.
[LAUGHS] While you were there, did you have any contact with the outside, knowing what was going on with the news at that point?
Oh, yes.
So like, we went-- I..
We- I met some very unusual people during those years.
And one person who I still think of a lot, who was very unusual.
We were like 10-- about 10 kilometers away from the nearest town, Macon, where we lived first.
And in Macon, there was a group, like I mentioned before.
There was a group of Jewish people we would always contact and we would stay in touch with.
One of these people was a former Russian man.
He was in his 40s.
He was a bachelor.
And he had lived there for about 20 or 25 years.
He had come from Russia.
And he lived there by himself.
He had a printing business.
He owned his own printing business, and he supported himself.
He was-- he had-- yeah, he was-- he had converted to Christian Science.
But he still considered itself part Jews-- part Jew.
He did not reject his Judaism.
He still had Jewish friends.
Because he had been very sick at one time.
He had tuberculosis, and the doctor had given up on him.
He had lost one lung.
And Christian Science saved his life.
So I can understand that.
So he became a Christian Scientist.
And he was one of the friends that my father had.
And he was a typical, I would say, Dostoevsky character, a man who lived only to help others.
And he had a radio.
And one day he asked my father, did you hear on the radio, such and such happened?
And my father says, I don't have a radio.
You know, couldn't buy a radio in those days.
You don't have a radio?
You have to have a radio.
He said-- he told my father, come home with me.
My father went home with him.
He says, here.
He takes this radio, just unplugs it, and gives it to my father.
My father said, you're- you're crazy.
I'm not going to take your radio.
It's your radio.
I want you to have my radio, he says.
I'm very offended if you don't take it.
And he- he- and he had to take it.
He forced him to.
We had this radio for many years.
Even after the war, we still had that radio.
So this is how we got-- how we had a radio.
We got the news.
And later this man also saved our life, literally.
What happened is one day-- my father had to register, I think, every month at the police station.
One day he comes home, completely devastated.
He says they kept our papers, confiscated the papers.
We have to be ready in 24 hours, blankets and food and such and such at the train station.
So we know what that meant.
And we said, oh my goodness.
You know, that was the end.
So he goes back.
I don't remember if it happened the same day or the following, or- He happened to go back downtown, or he was still downtown when it happened.
See, I don't know the detail.
Whatever it is, he met this man right after that happened.
And he says-- and he tells him, he says Mr.
Cohn, come with me.
I don't know if it's going to help, but at least I'm going to try.
And he tells him, walking over, he takes him to a friend of his.
He says, I know this guy.
I don't know exactly who he is.
His name is [?
Humm. ?] I don't know if it's real name.
I suspect that's not his real name.
He is taking Russian lessons from me.
I'm giving him-- I'm teaching him Russian.
I know-- I have a feeling this man is very influential.
He is a very big shot.
But I don't know anything about him.
Maybe he can do something.
I don't know.
I mean, you know, it's a desperate situation, so what have we got to lose?
He goes to see this man, and they sit.
My father says while he was sitting there waiting for this man to come in the living room, he sees these big maps from the battlefront, Germany or whatever was [INAUDIBLE].
He says, oh my god, for all I know he could be a German spy.
You know, who knows what he is?
He comes in, and this man introduces-- his name was [?
Hitrick, ?] Michael [?
Hitrick ?], this Russian guy.
He introduces my father and he tells him the whole story.
And he says please, can you-- is there anything you can do for this family, because it's a desperate situation.
The man says come with me.
They walk over to the-- to the-- to the police station where they had confiscated his papers, and he says-- and they stand to attention.
He walks in and they stand to attention.
He was in civilian clothes.
And he says, please give the papers back to this man and forget the-- and forget it ever happened.
And they did.
You may not believe it.
That's exactly what happened.

And later, when-- at the-- when the Germans-- no, the Germans toward the end, when they were fleeing the Allies, and they came-- then the German troops came through Macon, you know, the last week or so.
And the first place they went to was to arrest this man and his family.
It was the first thing.
But they had left already.
They couldn't get them.
He was a spy.
[BACKROUND NOISE] But maybe he was a double spy, or we don't know, because he spoke fluent German.
He learned Russian.
We still don't know who he was.
But he was a very influential man.
And through this man-- and another thing about this man, this Russian man, which is very remarkable and I want to mention here, sometime later, there was a rule.
All the Jews have to register there.
So it happened there.
It happened later, but eventually it happened.
And again my father met this man in the street.
He was in Macon.
And my father told us, I'm not going to register.
Cohn or not Cohn, he says-- again he says, if I register, I know we're dead.
I know it's a death sentence.
If I don't register, we still have a chance.
Maybe they won't-- they won't get us.
And he met this man in the street.
And the man says oh, I'm on my way to the police station to register.
And my father says you are going to register as a Jew?
You are not a Jew.
Why do you have to go?
You want to kill yourself?
Why-- why make it so easy for them?
You don't have to go.
He says Mr.
Cohn, you don't understand.
I know what's going to happen.
But I also know that wherever they-- they bring me, all the Jews who are there, they need all the help they can get.
And that's why I want to go.
And he got-- and he never came back.
And after the war, a common friend met my father.
And he says, I know you know Mr.
Hitrick ?] very well.
And I was in the same camp where he was, and he died there.
And he says, I want you to know this man was a saint.
He did exactly what he-- he sacrificed his life.
So you-- it brought the best and the worst out in people, you know?
Very strange.
Some people turned into monsters, turning in their own kind, trying to save their life.
And some people, they did what he did.
And like the French. you know.
The French who helped us survive, they knew what was at stake.
They knew it was dangerous.
Every single gesture, every single thing you did to help a Jew was very dangerous.
But they figured, it's no big deal.
That's what I have to do.
They listened to their conscience.
And some, you know, worked the other way around.
We're still during the war, like '44.
See, '44, already, there was like a glim of-- of hope.
Because in June was V Day.
And that to us was-- was a big-- was a very, very, very big event.
First we didn't believe it.
We didn't believe it really happened.
We got the news again through the British underground news, V Day, the debarquement.
And when we realized that they-- that they managed to stay-- because first, there was a fake debarquement at one time, of course, you remember, and it didn't work.
Whether it was intentional or not intentional, I don't know.
And then they came back in Normandy, and they actually managed to set foot.
And then we had-- my brother had these maps of France, and we made all these little flags.
And every time we listened to the news, he would change the flags.
And then it went faster, faster, faster, faster.
And it was like-- like- like magic.
I mean, you couldn't believe it, until one day they were there.
They came to our-- the debarquement, V Day, was in June, June 6.
And they were early, I think, the 8th of September.
And we were the first people to know they were there Because what happened was, like I just mentioned, the Germans would overrun the area.
And they were desperate.
And they were very dangerous at this time.
So we saw German troops for the first time.
And they would confiscate things, because to flee-- you know, they were running.
Every car, every bicycle, they took everything.
They confiscated everything.
They came to us.
They said, do you have a bicycle?
Do you have a horse?
They took anything they could get.
And they arrested a lot of people, shot them, you know, because they couldn't bother making them prisoner.
So they were just shot on the spot usually.
And so we figure it's not safe to sleep at home, to stay home at- at night, because you never know what can happen at night.
So we got permission from a farmer in another village a few miles away to stay in his barn, to sleep in his barn at night.
And it was very funny, because I remember the barn was crammed with mice.
We'd just sleep there, you know, with our clothes on.
And they would run over you and everything.
[LAUGHS] And so early in the morning, we would get up, and we would walk back to our village.
And it was still dark.
And we would, like, cross a mountain.
And down, we would look at the road.
There was like what we would call it highway now, it was like a national road, a highway.
National highway number six.
There was a road passing the village.
And we'd see all these trucks and trucks.
And it was very unusual, because you know, there was hardly any traffic in those days.
And we would look down and we figured those are Germans, because the Germans were leaving.
You know, we saw all these German trucks going.
And all of a sudden my father said, you know what, they don't look like German trucks.
Those are American.
And we looked and we say no, that's impossible.
And we look and sure enough, we see the Americans.
And we start screaming.
We say, oh my god, you know, they're here.
And we run.
We race as fast as we could run.
We run back to the village, and we wake up the whole village.
Everybody was still asleep.
It was maybe 5:00 in the morning or whatever.
We'd say the Allies are here, the Allies are here, the Allies are here, banging on the doors.
And they thought we were nuts.
This is it, you know, the poor-- the poor family, they're all crazy.
And finally they put some clothes on and they came outside, and sure enough.
And everybody was racing towards the road, you know, and screaming.
And then they made their little flags and things.
I mean, it was unbelievable.
And then nonstop, nonstop.
Day and night, nonstop.
Then we realized what equipment they had.
It never stopped for a second.
Trucks, tanks, trucks, tanks, you know, just going.
And they would go faster and faster.
And it took only a short time before all of France was- was-- was liberated and whatever.
And then you know, in Paris, I mean, they were hysterical-- hysterical when the Allies came.
Of course the-- the Americans were very fortunate, because they had all these gorgeous girls, you know, and they would kiss them, and they were all over them, you know.
And they would sure take advantage of it.
It was September, fall.
Fall of '44, yes.
Oh, everything of course changed for the better.
We even got a visit.
American-- American soldiers and officers came occasionally to visit us.
It was a very remote place.
Because they checked out, are there any Jews here?
And then they came and they brought us some-- some food and some stuff, you know, just to visit.
Because they wanted to know if there were still Jews who had survived.
So it happened at least a couple of times.
Even a relative, a long lost relative who lived in the United States had managed to find us.
And my brother actually got married.
It's another story.
He was in the army, and he married somebody from the American army too.
And my brother went to the wedding.
They got married in Paris at the time.
And we didn't even know he was related to us, a Cohn, you know, far back relative.
So you know, a lot of-- I mean, suddenly the fear was gone, you know?
Things didn't turn to normal right away, but the fear was gone.
You could finally say OK, I'm alive, you know?
And you could relax.
And then of course, gradually, you discovered what had happened, you know?
And all the people who didn't make it.
And my father's family was practically wiped out.
All my friends, you know, were all dead, school friends.
Hardly anybody had survived.
And when we finally came-- somebody mentioned to my father, he says you know, you're the first family-- Jewish family-- I see where the four members are still around-- father, mother, daughter.
It was very unusual.
Either the children were dead, the parents were dead.
I mean, I can give you stories.
Like one of my best friends, she was sent with a group to-- from Belgium to Switzerland for safety.
When she came back, her parents were gone, never came back.
And there were, you know-- I worked after-- we still lived in France.
I was in Paris to- to take a-- to take a class, a private class of fashion illustration.
I was-- for a while I did fashion illustration, fashion designing in Paris.
And I was working with a famous fashion artist at the time.
And for room and board, I stayed-- the [INAUDIBLE] actually provided my scholarship for that.
And I stayed-- for room and board, I worked at an orphanage, a Jewish orphanage in Paris that was sponsored by Mrs.
Rothschild, the Rothschild family.
They were all girls, a girl orphanage, all from the Holocaust, orphans.
The youngest was two and the oldest was 16.
And it was crowded.
I don't know how many there were.
And I was in charge of looking after one group of-- of girls.
I was 18.
That was the same year in fall we went back to Belgium.
We were still in France at that time.
And but to say, you know, a lot of children survived, but the parents were dead.
And then they tried to get them to be adopted, you know, by families.
So there were orphans or parents without children, children without parents.
All families were cut like this.
And you know, of course, many families were completely wiped out.
No, no, Belgium, to Antwerp.
See, Hamburg was-- that was over, you know?
Actually I'm going to go there next month, first time in-- [LAUGHS] But we were, you know-- I mean, we considered Belgium.
My father actually got an offer after the war from the German government.
They were very anxious, you know, to-- to look good.
And they were very-- Adenauer was in charge after the war.
He was a very good guy.
He was actually in jail many years by the Nazis.
And they wanted-- they offered my father to do anything.
You know, pay-- all expenses paid, move us back to Hamburg, get him an office, get us a house, anything.
And my father said no, I can't do it.
He had planned to go back.
That's why he went to Belgium at the time.
He didn't want to go too far, because he figured, you know, a couple of years, craziness, I'll go back.
But he says no, I can't go back anymore after what happened.
You know, his family was all wiped out.
He says if I shake hands with somebody, I don't know if it's not a murderer of somebody of my family.
You know, you can't-- that was right after the war.
Now of course it's two, three generations later.
Things are a little different.
But he couldn't.
And yet he was very homesick.
I know that.
He never admitted it, but I know that he was very homesick all these years.
But--
Yes.
I was 18 then.
No.
But we had, as I mentioned before, we had left all our furniture and everything in that-- in that warehouse.
And so we got most of it back, which was also a blessing.
And so we rented a house.
Actually my brother went back first, and he made all the arrangements.
So he rented a house for us in a suburb from Antwerp, and we stayed there.
I stayed there until I came here in 1962.
So then I went to the-- my dream, one of my big dreams came true.
I went to the Art Academy in Antwerp, which is a fantastic, huge art school.
It's very hard to get in.
And I made it.
And I-- my entire childhood I was dreaming about this.
Someday I'm going to go to the Art Institute.
They have students from all over the world there.
There was actually an American and a Canadian in my class at the time.
And then of course, it was time I had to make a living, unfortunately.
And then I started working.
I taught myself shorthand and typing, and I started working as a secretary.
And I ended up-- it was a very nice job, you know, very big water-- you know, barge shipping company, international.
And actually I'm getting a pension from them now, from Belgium.
It's not a huge pension, but it's a pension.
And so I supported myself.
And I-- you know, gradually life went back to normal.
My father passed away of leukemia in '51.
And you know, according to the doctor, had something to do-- I mean, the stress of all these years, of course, took its toll.
And then my mother-- my brother get married.
And I lived with my mother until I came here in 1962.
I had only planned to come here for a year.
Here, in this country.
Yes.
So this is not connected with the Holocaust really, because Belgium is a nice country.
It's very-- very pleasant to live there.
Yeah, I came here, and I met-- I met Bob, my husband, and we got married.
So that's how it happened.
I actually came to learn the language.
I wanted to add you know, English to my languages.
So that's, in a nutshell, you know, what happened.
First I was in New York for two months and worked there.
Then I came to Chicago.
I had a cousin here and I came to visit her, and she convinced me to stay.
And I first worked here at the Heinemann Studios as an artist, fashion-- fashion Illustrator, doing fashion illustration, which was fun.
But it's-- unfortunately, it's kind of a dying art.
It's not much done anymore, you know.
Photography has replaced it mostly.
Then I worked as a secretary at the University of Chicago for a professor, a theologian named Paul Tillich.
I mean, he's quite well known, still is, even though he died many years ago.
He was from Germany originally.
Not Jewish. but he was against the Nazi government.
He was almost killed.
He was close to being killed as a result, and so he had emigrated to the United States.
And he was the only professor at divinity school that was entitled to have a private secretary, and I was the one that was hired.
I was very fortunate.
So I worked with him until my first child was born, my daughter, my eldest daughter.
Jacqueline.
We named her Jackie.
I had another child almost three years later, yes.
So I have two daughters.

Well, I would say when I-- when I see how things can degenerate between humans who-- who own the power and who misuse it in a most horrible and cruel way, I became even more concerned about protecting the weak and the powerless than I had ever before.
And I go all the way, you know, children, women, those who have no power, and animals, you know, cruelty to animals too.
I'm even an animal-- I could call myself an animal activist, and I'm a vegetarian.
So I-- I went all the way, me and my daughters.
And-
